course_info.title: Advanced Network Security
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Evaluate next-generation firewall capabilities beyond traditional packet filtering
Design multi-tier firewall architectures for enterprise environments
Configure application-layer inspection and deep packet inspection policies
Analyze firewall performance tradeoffs when enabling advanced features
readings: Network Security Through Data Analysis, 2nd Ed. Ch. 8-9
NIST SP 800-41 Rev. 1 - Guidelines on Firewalls and Firewall Policy
Palo Alto Networks NGFW Architecture Whitepaper

lecture_notes.introduction: Traditional firewalls operated at layers 3 and 4 of the OSI model, making allow-or-deny decisions based on source and destination IP addresses, port numbers, and protocol type. While effective for simple perimeter defense, this approach is fundamentally blind to the content of network traffic. Modern enterprise networks demand far more granular inspection. Next-generation firewalls (NGFWs) integrate application-layer awareness, user identity correlation, intrusion prevention, TLS decryption, and threat intelligence feeds into a single platform. This session examines the architectural evolution from stateless packet filters to stateful inspection to application-aware NGFWs. We will analyze how these systems decompose traffic into application flows, apply policy based on user identity rather than IP address, and leverage sandboxing to detonate suspicious files inline. Understanding the internal architecture of NGFWs is critical for designing firewall policies that balance security visibility with network performance, and for recognizing the inherent limitations that attackers exploit to bypass even the most advanced firewalls.
key_concepts: Evolution from packet filters to stateful inspection to NGFWs: each generation adds visibility into higher protocol layers while maintaining backward compatibility with lower-layer filtering
Application-layer identification using protocol decoding, SSL decryption, and behavioral heuristics rather than relying solely on port numbers, enabling policy enforcement regardless of port evasion
User-identity-based firewall policies integrating with Active Directory, LDAP, or SAML to apply rules based on who the user is, not just which IP address they occupy
Deep packet inspection (DPI) mechanics: reassembling fragmented packets, decompressing payloads, and inspecting content against signature databases and behavioral patterns
TLS/SSL inspection architecture: the firewall terminates TLS, inspects plaintext, then re-encrypts to the destination, requiring trusted CA certificate deployment across the organization
Multi-zone firewall design: DMZ, internal, management, guest, and IoT zones with explicit inter-zone policies following the principle of least privilege for lateral traffic
Firewall high-availability configurations: active-passive, active-active, and clustered designs with session state synchronization to prevent connection drops during failover
Performance considerations: enabling DPI, TLS inspection, and sandboxing degrades throughput by 40-70% versus raw packet forwarding; capacity planning must account for worst-case inspection load
Firewall evasion techniques: protocol tunneling (DNS tunneling, HTTP/S encapsulation), fragmentation attacks, and slow-rate application-layer attacks that bypass timeout-based state tracking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploying a pfSense firewall with Suricata IPS integration in a virtualized lab, configuring application-aware rules to differentiate between HTTPS web browsing and HTTPS-tunneled SSH traffic, then demonstrating how DNS-over-HTTPS traffic bypasses traditional DNS-based filtering and how to detect it
setup_requirements: VMware or VirtualBox with pfSense VM and two client VMs
Suricata IPS with ET Open ruleset installed
Wireshark for traffic capture analysis
DNS-over-HTTPS testing tools (doh-client)

review.value: Traditional firewalls see packets; NGFWs see applications and users. A traditional firewall allows TCP/443; an NGFW distinguishes between HTTPS to a banking site, HTTPS tunneling SSH, and HTTPS carrying malware. This application awareness is the foundation of modern perimeter security.

Without TLS inspection, the firewall is blind to 90%+ of traffic. With it, you break end-to-end encryption, create a single point of decryption vulnerability, and may violate privacy regulations. Best practice: inspect corporate traffic, exempt banking/healthcare/personal sites, and protect the inspection keys rigorously.

Flat networks allow unrestricted lateral movement after a breach. Zone-based firewall design creates internal boundaries that limit blast radius. Each zone-to-zone traffic flow must have an explicit policy. Default inter-zone policy should be deny-all.

A firewall rated at 10 Gbps for packet forwarding may deliver only 3 Gbps with DPI and TLS inspection enabled. Always size firewalls based on worst-case inspection throughput, not marketing throughput numbers. Monitor CPU and session table utilization continuously.

Attackers tunnel C2 traffic through DNS, encode data in HTTP headers, or use cloud services as relay points. Firewalls must combine signature detection with behavioral analysis (beacon timing, data volume anomalies) to catch evasion techniques.

learning_objectives: Compare signature-based, anomaly-based, and behavioral detection approaches
Deploy and tune Suricata IPS with custom rule creation
Evaluate detection effectiveness using metrics including precision, recall, and F1 score
Design IDS/IPS architecture for encrypted traffic environments
readings: Practical Intrusion Analysis: Prevention and Detection for the Twenty-First Century, Ch. 3-5
Suricata User Guide - Rule Writing Documentation
MITRE ATT&CK Technique Coverage Analysis for IDS Rulesets

lecture_notes.introduction: Intrusion detection and prevention systems form the core of network-based threat detection. First-generation IDS relied on matching known attack signatures against network traffic, a reactive approach that was effective against known threats but useless against novel attacks. Modern IDS/IPS platforms combine multiple detection paradigms: signature matching catches known bad patterns, statistical anomaly detection identifies deviations from normal baselines, and behavioral analysis models track sequences of actions that match attack kill chains even when individual actions appear benign. The distinction between IDS (detection and alerting) and IPS (inline blocking) determines placement architecture. IDS operates on a network tap or SPAN port, analyzing copies of traffic without affecting flow. IPS sits inline, able to drop malicious packets in real-time but introducing latency and the risk of blocking legitimate traffic on false positives. This session covers the internal architecture of modern detection engines, focusing on Suricata as an open-source platform that supports multi-threaded packet processing, protocol-aware inspection, file extraction, and Lua scripting for custom detection logic. We examine how to write effective rules, tune detection to minimize false positives while maintaining high detection rates, and architect IDS/IPS deployments that provide visibility even when most traffic is encrypted.
key_concepts: Signature-based detection: pattern matching against known malicious byte sequences, effective for known threats with near-zero false positives but blind to zero-day attacks and evasion variants
Anomaly detection: establishing statistical baselines for traffic volume, protocol distribution, and connection patterns, then alerting on deviations that exceed configurable thresholds

Behavioral detection: modeling multi-step attack sequences where individual actions may be benign but the sequence matches known attack kill chains (e.g., reconnaissance then exploitation then lateral movement)
Suricata rule anatomy: action (alert/drop), protocol, source/destination, direction operator, and detection options including content matching, PCRE, byte_test, and flowbits for stateful detection

Rule performance tuning: ordering content matches from most specific to least specific, using fast_pattern to optimize multi-pattern matching, and avoiding overly broad rules that cause CPU exhaustion
Detection metrics: true positive rate (recall), precision, F1 score, and the ROC curve for evaluating threshold sensitivity tradeoffs between detection completeness and false positive volume
IPS deployment modes: inline bridging (transparent), routed inline, and TAP-mode with active response; each has different failure modes and network architecture implications

Encrypted traffic challenges: JA3/JA3S fingerprinting of TLS handshakes, certificate metadata analysis, and encrypted traffic analytics (ETA) using flow-level features when content inspection is impossible
Multi-sensor architecture: distributing IDS sensors at network ingress, between security zones, and at cloud connectivity points to provide layered visibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing custom Suricata rules to detect a simulated command-and-control beacon using JA3 TLS fingerprinting and flow-based detection, then running the rules against a PCAP containing mixed legitimate and malicious traffic to calculate detection rate and false positive rate
setup_requirements: Suricata 7.x installed on a Linux VM
ET Open and custom rule files
PCAP datasets containing labeled malicious traffic
JA3 fingerprint database for comparison

review.value: Signatures catch known attacks with high precision. Behavioral detection catches novel attacks but generates more false positives. Production deployments combine both: signatures for known threats with high-confidence automated blocking, behavioral rules for unknown threats with analyst investigation.

JA3 hashes the TLS ClientHello parameters (cipher suites, extensions, elliptic curves) into a fingerprint that identifies the TLS client implementation. Malware often has a distinctive JA3 fingerprint that differs from legitimate browsers, enabling detection even when content is encrypted.

A new IDS deployment generates thousands of alerts daily. Tuning is iterative: suppress known false positives, threshold noisy rules, and create exceptions for legitimate traffic patterns. The goal is a manageable alert queue where every alert warrants investigation.

Inline IPS can fail-open (pass all traffic when the engine crashes, losing security) or fail-closed (block all traffic, causing an outage). The choice depends on whether security or availability is the higher priority for each network segment.

Map IDS rules to MITRE ATT&CK techniques to identify gaps. If you have rules for Initial Access and Execution but none for Lateral Movement or Exfiltration, you have blind spots in the kill chain that attackers will exploit.

learning_objectives: Analyze the security implications of separating control and data planes
Implement dynamic microsegmentation policies using SDN controllers
Identify SDN-specific attack vectors including controller compromise and flow table poisoning
Design resilient SDN architectures that mitigate single points of failure
readings: SDN: Software Defined Networks by Nadeau and Gray, Ch. 8-12
OpenFlow Specification v1.5 Security Considerations
NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2 - Developing Cyber-Resilient Systems

lecture_notes.introduction: Software-defined networking fundamentally restructures how networks are built and managed by separating the control plane (the intelligence that decides where traffic goes) from the data plane (the hardware that forwards packets). In traditional networks, every router and switch contains its own control logic, making coordinated security policy changes slow and error-prone. SDN centralizes this intelligence in a controller that has a global view of the entire network topology and can programmatically modify forwarding rules across all switches simultaneously. For security, this is transformative: an SDN controller can implement microsegmentation at the individual workload level, instantly quarantine a compromised host by modifying flow rules, redirect suspicious traffic to a sandbox for analysis, and enforce dynamic access policies based on real-time threat intelligence. However, this centralization also creates new risks. The SDN controller becomes the single most critical asset in the network. If compromised, an attacker gains the ability to redirect, intercept, or block any network traffic at will. The southbound API between controller and switches (typically OpenFlow) becomes an attack surface. Flow table capacity limits on physical switches can be exploited for denial-of-service. This session examines both the defensive power and the attack surface of SDN, providing the knowledge to architect SDN deployments that maximize security benefits while mitigating the risks inherent in centralized network control.
key_concepts: SDN architecture: application layer (security apps), control layer (controller with global network view), and infrastructure layer (switches executing flow rules) connected by northbound and southbound APIs
OpenFlow protocol: controller pushes flow rules to switches defining match criteria and actions (forward, drop, mirror, modify); flow rules are evaluated in priority order in hardware flow tables

Microsegmentation via SDN: defining per-workload firewall policies at the switch level rather than at network boundaries, allowing policies like 'web server A can talk to database B on port 5432 only'
Dynamic quarantine: when a threat is detected, the SDN controller instantly modifies flow rules to isolate the compromised host while maintaining forensic logging of its traffic for investigation

SDN controller security: hardening the controller OS, encrypting the southbound API with TLS, implementing role-based access control for northbound API calls, and deploying controller clusters for redundancy
Flow table poisoning: an attacker who can inject flow rules (via a compromised switch or controller API) can redirect traffic to an interception point or create covert exfiltration channels
Controller DoS attacks: overwhelming the controller with packet-in events from crafted traffic that does not match any existing flow rule, forcing the controller to process each packet individually
Network Function Virtualization (NFV): deploying security functions (firewall, IDS, DLP) as virtual appliances that SDN traffic steering chains together based on policy
East-west traffic visibility: in traditional networks, traffic between servers on the same VLAN never touches a firewall; SDN enables inspection of all traffic regardless of topology
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using Mininet to emulate a data center network with an ONOS SDN controller, implementing microsegmentation policies that restrict lateral movement between application tiers, then demonstrating real-time quarantine by automatically isolating a simulated compromised host when Suricata detects malicious activity
setup_requirements: Mininet SDN emulator with Open vSwitch
ONOS or OpenDaylight SDN controller
Python scripts for flow rule management
Suricata integrated for threat detection triggering

review.value: Traditional firewalls protect the perimeter. Once an attacker is inside, they move laterally unimpeded. Microsegmentation applies firewall rules at every workload boundary, so compromising one server does not grant access to others. This is the foundation of zero-trust networking.

The SDN controller is simultaneously the most powerful defensive tool and the highest-value target. It must be treated like a domain controller: hardened OS, minimal services, encrypted communications, multi-factor authentication, audit logging, and physical security.

Physical switches have finite TCAM memory for flow tables (typically 2,000-16,000 entries). Microsegmentation at scale can exhaust flow tables. Design must use hierarchical policies and wildcards to minimize table entries while maintaining granular control.

SDN can steer traffic through a sequence of virtual security appliances (firewall then IDS then DLP) based on policy. This enables flexible security architectures where new functions can be inserted without re-cabling the network.

In data centers, 80% of traffic is east-west (server to server) and never crosses a perimeter firewall. SDN provides the only practical way to enforce security policies on this traffic at scale without deploying physical appliances on every link.

learning_objectives: Design a zero trust architecture following NIST SP 800-207 principles
Implement policy enforcement points and policy decision points
Integrate identity, device posture, and context into access decisions
Evaluate commercial and open-source zero trust solutions
readings: NIST SP 800-207 - Zero Trust Architecture
Google BeyondCorp papers (original 2014 and BeyondCorp Enterprise)
CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model v2.0

lecture_notes.introduction: The zero trust security model eliminates the concept of a trusted internal network. In traditional perimeter-based security, users and devices inside the corporate network are implicitly trusted, and security controls focus on keeping threats out. This model has proven inadequate as cloud adoption, remote work, and sophisticated attackers who achieve initial access make the perimeter meaningless. Zero trust operates on the principle of never trust, always verify. Every access request, whether from inside or outside the network, must be authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated before granting access to resources. The architecture requires three core components: a strong identity foundation that verifies who the user is and what device they are using, a policy engine that evaluates access requests against contextual attributes (identity, device health, location, time, data sensitivity), and policy enforcement points deployed at every resource boundary to mediate access. Google pioneered this approach with BeyondCorp, which treats the corporate network as untrusted and routes all access through an identity-aware proxy. NIST SP 800-207 provides a vendor-neutral framework for implementing zero trust. This session covers the practical engineering challenges of deploying zero trust: migrating from perimeter-based to identity-based access, handling legacy applications that cannot participate in modern authentication, and the organizational change management required to shift from network-centric to identity-centric security.
key_concepts: Zero trust core principles: verify explicitly (authenticate and authorize every request), use least-privilege access (just-in-time and just-enough access), and assume breach (minimize blast radius, segment access, verify end-to-end encryption)

NIST SP 800-207 architecture components: Policy Engine (PE) makes access decisions, Policy Administrator (PA) establishes/terminates connections, Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) mediates all resource access
Identity-centric access: replacing network location (VPN, IP range) as the basis for trust with verified identity, device health attestation, and behavioral context as the access decision inputs
Device posture assessment: verifying OS patch level, endpoint protection status, disk encryption, jailbreak detection, and certificate validity before granting access to sensitive resources
Continuous authorization: access is not granted once and forgotten; the policy engine continuously re-evaluates conditions and can revoke access mid-session if risk conditions change
Micro-perimeters: each application or data resource has its own access policy enforced at the resource boundary, eliminating the assumption that adjacent resources share the same trust level
Google BeyondCorp implementation: access proxy architecture where all employee access to internal applications goes through an identity-aware proxy regardless of network location
Legacy application integration: reverse proxies, application wrapping, and network-level micro-segmentation as bridging strategies for applications that cannot support modern authentication protocols
CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model: five pillars (Identity, Devices, Networks, Applications/Workloads, Data) across four maturity levels (Traditional, Initial, Advanced, Optimal) for planning migration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring an identity-aware access proxy using open-source tools (Pomerium or oauth2-proxy) that enforces per-application access policies based on user identity and device posture, demonstrating how the same user is granted different access levels depending on whether they connect from a managed corporate laptop versus a personal device
setup_requirements: Pomerium or oauth2-proxy deployed on Docker
Identity provider (Keycloak) with test users and groups
Two sample web applications with different sensitivity levels
Device posture simulation scripts

review.value: Zero trust does not mean zero access. It means every access request is evaluated on its merits: who is the user, what is the device state, what resource is being accessed, and what is the risk context. The goal is informed trust decisions, not blanket denial.

VPN provides network-level access: once connected, the user can reach everything on the network. Zero trust provides application-level access: each application has its own policy. Compromising a VPN gives broad access; compromising a zero trust session gives access only to the specific application.

Organizations cannot switch to zero trust overnight. Start with high-value applications, implement identity-aware access proxies, and gradually reduce reliance on network-based trust. Run perimeter and zero trust controls in parallel during transition.

Zero trust sessions are not fire-and-forget. If a device fails a health check mid-session, or if user behavior becomes anomalous, the policy engine should re-evaluate and potentially terminate the session. This requires real-time signal collection from identity, endpoint, and network sources.

Ultimately, zero trust is about protecting data, not networks. Classify data by sensitivity, apply policies based on data classification, and ensure encryption and access controls follow the data regardless of where it resides (on-premises, cloud, SaaS).

learning_objectives: Design microsegmentation policies using application dependency mapping
Implement host-based microsegmentation using agent-based and agentless approaches
Develop migration strategies from flat networks to segmented architectures
Measure microsegmentation effectiveness through lateral movement testing
readings: Zero Trust Networks, 2nd Ed. by Gilman and Barth, Ch. 6-8
Illumio/VMware NSX microsegmentation architecture guides
NIST SP 800-125B - Secure Virtual Network Configuration for VM Protection

lecture_notes.introduction: Microsegmentation extends the zero trust principle to its logical conclusion: every workload operates within its own security boundary with explicitly defined communication policies. Traditional network segmentation uses VLANs and firewalls to create coarse-grained zones (DMZ, internal, production, development). While better than flat networks, these zones still allow unrestricted lateral movement within each zone. When an attacker compromises a single server in the production zone, they can freely communicate with every other server in that zone. Microsegmentation eliminates this by defining allowed communication paths at the individual workload level. A web server can communicate with its specific database server on its specific port, and nothing else. Implementation approaches vary: network-based microsegmentation uses SDN or cloud security groups to enforce policies at the switch level, while host-based microsegmentation deploys agents on each workload that enforce policies in the host firewall. Each approach has tradeoffs in visibility, performance, and management complexity. The critical first step in any microsegmentation project is application dependency mapping. Before you can write policies that define what is allowed, you must understand what communication currently occurs. This requires observing traffic flows, often for weeks, to build a complete picture of application dependencies. Writing policies without this understanding will break applications and generate immediate rollback pressure from operations teams.
key_concepts: Application dependency mapping: observing and documenting all communication flows between workloads to understand what traffic is legitimate before writing restriction policies

Host-based microsegmentation: agents on each workload enforce firewall policies locally, providing consistent enforcement across physical, virtual, and cloud environments without depending on network infrastructure

Network-based microsegmentation: using SDN flow rules or cloud security groups to enforce policies at the network layer, providing enforcement without installing agents but limited to environments where the network infrastructure supports it
Policy modeling: starting in observe-only mode to detect policy violations without blocking, then progressively tightening to enforcement mode as confidence in policy completeness grows

Workload identity: labeling workloads by function (web, app, database), environment (production, staging, development), and application rather than by IP address, enabling policies that survive workload migration

Lateral movement containment: microsegmentation limits the blast radius of a compromise by preventing an attacker on a compromised web server from reaching the database server, even though both are on the same network segment
Ring-fencing critical assets: applying the strictest microsegmentation policies to high-value targets (domain controllers, databases, key management systems) as the highest-priority first phase

Operational challenges: policy management at scale (thousands of workloads), handling dynamic environments (auto-scaling, container orchestration), and maintaining policies through application updates

Compliance alignment: microsegmentation directly supports PCI DSS requirement for cardholder data environment isolation, HIPAA network segmentation requirements, and regulatory expectations for defense in depth
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using a host-based microsegmentation tool (Illumio or open-source equivalent) to map application dependencies in a multi-tier lab environment, define allow-list policies, switch from observe to enforce mode, and then attempt lateral movement from a simulated compromised host to verify that segmentation policies block unauthorized communication
setup_requirements: Multi-tier lab environment with web, application, and database VMs
Host-based firewall management tool or iptables automation scripts
Traffic flow monitoring (tcpdump or NetFlow collection)
Lateral movement testing tools (Metasploit or CrackMapExec)

review.value: The number one cause of microsegmentation project failure is writing policies without understanding existing traffic flows. Observe mode for 2-4 weeks captures legitimate communication patterns. Missing a critical dependency means breaking production when enforcement begins.

Microsegmentation uses allow-list policies: only explicitly permitted communication is allowed, and everything else is denied. This is the opposite of traditional firewalls that deny known bad and allow everything else. Allow-listing provides far stronger security but requires complete knowledge of legitimate traffic.

Policies based on workload labels (role=web-server, env=production, app=payment) survive infrastructure changes like VM migration, IP reassignment, and auto-scaling. IP-based policies break whenever addresses change and do not scale to dynamic environments.

Phase 1: Ring-fence critical assets (domain controllers, databases). Phase 2: Segment between application tiers. Phase 3: Segment within tiers. Phase 4: Segment individual workloads. Each phase provides incremental security improvement while managing operational risk.

Microsegmentation is only as good as its testing. Regular lateral movement exercises (red team or automated) should verify that segmentation policies actually prevent unauthorized communication. Policy drift and exceptions accumulate over time without testing.

learning_objectives: Execute and defend against advanced wireless attacks including KRACK, Dragonblood, and evil twin attacks
Implement enterprise wireless security with 802.1X/EAP and RADIUS
Deploy wireless intrusion detection and prevention systems
Design secure wireless architecture for enterprise environments with BYOD policies
readings: 802.11 Wireless Networks: The Definitive Guide, 3rd Ed. Ch. 10-14
NIST SP 800-153 - Guidelines for Securing WLANs
WPA3 Specification and Dragonblood vulnerability analysis papers

lecture_notes.introduction: Wireless networks present unique security challenges because the transmission medium, radio waves, cannot be physically contained. Any attacker within radio range can receive and potentially inject packets, making wireless inherently more exposed than wired networks. The evolution of wireless security from WEP (broken within minutes) to WPA2 (vulnerable to KRACK and PMKID attacks) to WPA3 (addressing known weaknesses but introducing new ones like Dragonblood) reflects an ongoing arms race between protocol designers and attackers. Enterprise wireless security goes beyond choosing the right encryption protocol. It requires certificate-based authentication using 802.1X with EAP-TLS to prevent credential theft, network segmentation to isolate wireless clients from critical infrastructure, wireless intrusion detection to identify rogue access points and attack tools, and physical layer security considerations including radio frequency planning to minimize signal leakage beyond building boundaries. Modern enterprises face additional complexity from bring-your-own-device (BYOD) policies, IoT devices that may not support modern authentication protocols, and guest wireless networks that must provide internet access without exposing the corporate network. This session covers the full spectrum of wireless threats and defenses, from the cryptographic details of protocol attacks to the architectural decisions that determine whether a wireless deployment is a security liability or a properly managed access medium.
key_concepts: WPA3-Personal (SAE): Simultaneous Authentication of Equals replaces PSK four-way handshake, providing forward secrecy and resistance to offline dictionary attacks against captured handshakes
WPA3-Enterprise (192-bit): mandatory Suite B cryptography with GCMP-256, HMAC-SHA384, and ECDSA-384 for environments requiring the highest wireless security levels

KRACK attack mechanics: key reinstallation attacks exploit the WPA2 four-way handshake by replaying message 3, causing the client to reinstall an already-used key with a reset nonce, enabling decryption
Dragonblood vulnerabilities: side-channel attacks against WPA3-SAE that leak information about the password during the Dragonfly handshake, enabling offline dictionary attacks on weak passwords
802.1X/EAP-TLS enterprise authentication: using client certificates for mutual authentication between device and RADIUS server, eliminating password-based attacks entirely

Evil twin attacks: attacker creates a rogue access point mimicking a legitimate network, capturing credentials or conducting man-in-the-middle attacks; defense requires 802.1X mutual authentication and WIDS
Wireless IDS/IPS (WIDS/WIPS): dedicated sensors monitoring the RF spectrum for rogue access points, client deauthentication attacks, and anomalous wireless activity patterns

RF containment and site survey: planning access point placement and power levels to provide coverage within the facility while minimizing signal leakage to adjacent areas where attackers could operate
BYOD segmentation: dynamic VLAN assignment based on device type, posture assessment, and user role, isolating personal devices from corporate resources while allowing internet access

IoT wireless challenges: many IoT devices support only WPA2-PSK or older protocols, requiring dedicated IoT VLANs with strict network-level access controls to compensate for weak device-level security
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a controlled wireless lab with a legitimate WPA3-Enterprise access point and a FreeRADIUS server, then conducting an evil twin attack using hostapd to demonstrate credential capture against WPA2-PSK clients, followed by showing how 802.1X/EAP-TLS authentication prevents the attack because the rogue access point cannot present a valid server certificate
setup_requirements: Two wireless adapters supporting monitor mode and packet injection
FreeRADIUS server with EAP-TLS certificate infrastructure
hostapd for rogue access point creation
Wireshark with 802.11 dissector for wireless packet analysis

review.value: WPA3 fixes the major weaknesses of WPA2 (offline handshake attacks, KRACK) but introduces new complexity. Dragonblood showed that even well-designed protocols can have implementation vulnerabilities. Defense in depth remains essential: strong authentication, segmentation, and monitoring.

802.1X with EAP-TLS uses certificates for authentication, eliminating the entire class of password-based attacks (dictionary attacks, credential stuffing, phishing for wireless passwords). The operational cost of certificate management is justified by the security improvement for enterprise networks.

A rogue access point on the corporate network bypasses all perimeter security controls. WIDS sensors continuously scan for unauthorized access points. Critical defense includes 802.1X mutual authentication so clients reject connections to access points that cannot prove their identity.

Wireless signals do not stop at building walls. An attacker in the parking lot can reach internal networks. Directional antennas, power management, and RF shielding reduce the attack surface. Site surveys should map signal coverage and identify leakage points.

IoT devices with weak or no authentication must be isolated on dedicated VLANs with network-level access controls. The IoT VLAN should have no route to corporate resources, only to the specific cloud services each device requires. Monitor IoT traffic for anomalies indicating compromise.

learning_objectives: Assess security risks specific to IoT network protocols including MQTT, CoAP, and Zigbee
Design network architectures that isolate IoT devices from critical infrastructure
Implement monitoring and anomaly detection for IoT network traffic
Develop IoT security policies addressing device lifecycle from provisioning to decommissioning
readings: Practical IoT Hacking by Fotios Chantzis et al., Ch. 5-8
NIST SP 800-183 - Networks of Things
OWASP IoT Top 10 2024

lecture_notes.introduction: The Internet of Things has expanded the network attack surface by orders of magnitude. By 2025, an estimated 75 billion IoT devices are connected globally, many with minimal security capabilities. Unlike traditional IT devices that receive regular security updates and run modern operating systems, IoT devices frequently use stripped-down embedded operating systems, run firmware that is rarely updated, use hardcoded credentials, communicate over insecure protocols, and have no mechanism for endpoint security agents. From a network security perspective, IoT devices pose a dual challenge: they are easy to compromise and can be used as pivot points into the broader network, and they are difficult to secure at the device level because manufacturers prioritize cost and time-to-market over security. The network must compensate for device-level security weaknesses by providing strong segmentation, traffic monitoring, and access control. IoT network protocols introduce their own security considerations. MQTT, widely used for IoT messaging, supports TLS and authentication but many deployments leave these disabled for performance reasons. CoAP, designed as a lightweight HTTP alternative for constrained devices, uses DTLS for security but many implementations omit it. Zigbee and Z-Wave mesh networking protocols have their own encryption schemes with varying strength. This session covers the strategies for securing IoT at the network level when you cannot trust the devices themselves, building defense architectures that contain the damage when an IoT device is inevitably compromised.
key_concepts: IoT attack surface: hardcoded credentials, unencrypted communications, lack of firmware update mechanisms, debug interfaces left enabled, and insecure default configurations that users never change

MQTT security: enabling TLS encryption, implementing username/password or certificate-based authentication, and using access control lists to restrict which topics each device can publish to or subscribe to
CoAP and DTLS: Constrained Application Protocol uses UDP for lightweight communication; DTLS provides encryption but adds overhead that some constrained devices cannot handle, creating a security gap

Zigbee and Z-Wave security: mesh networking protocols with AES-128 encryption but vulnerable to key interception during pairing, replay attacks, and insufficient authentication in some implementations
Network segmentation for IoT: dedicated IoT VLANs or subnets with firewall rules restricting IoT-to-corporate traffic, allowing only specific cloud service destinations required by each device class
IoT traffic profiling: establishing baseline communication patterns for each device type (expected destination IPs, port numbers, data volumes, communication intervals) and alerting on deviations
IoT gateway security: deploying protocol-aware gateways that terminate IoT protocols, inspect and filter messages, and relay sanitized data to corporate systems, acting as a security boundary

Device inventory and lifecycle management: maintaining a complete inventory of all IoT devices, their firmware versions, known vulnerabilities, and patch status; establishing decommissioning procedures
Supply chain risks: IoT devices may contain backdoors from manufacturers, compromised firmware from supply chain attacks, or pre-installed malware; verification procedures must be part of procurement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up an MQTT broker with and without TLS/authentication, demonstrating how an attacker can subscribe to all topics on an unprotected broker to intercept sensor data, inject false commands, and compromise device operations, then showing how proper TLS, authentication, and ACL configuration prevents these attacks
setup_requirements: Mosquitto MQTT broker with TLS configuration
MQTT client tools (mosquitto_sub, mosquitto_pub, MQTT Explorer)
Simulated IoT device publishing sensor data
Network traffic analyzer for IoT protocol inspection

review.value: You cannot install antivirus on a smart lightbulb. The network must provide the security that the device cannot: segment IoT devices, monitor their traffic, and restrict their communication to only what is necessary. The network is the security control plane for IoT.

An MQTT broker without authentication allows any client to subscribe to all topics and publish to any topic. In an industrial setting, this means an attacker could read sensor data or send commands to actuators. Always enable TLS and authentication, even on internal networks.

IoT devices have predictable communication patterns: a temperature sensor sends readings every 60 seconds to one cloud endpoint. If it suddenly starts scanning the local network or connecting to unfamiliar IP addresses, it is compromised. Baseline-based anomaly detection is highly effective for IoT.

Devices that cannot receive firmware updates are permanently vulnerable once a vulnerability is discovered. Procurement policies should require OTA update capability and vendor commitment to security patches for a defined support lifetime.

IoT devices that are decommissioned but not wiped may contain WiFi credentials, API keys, and network configuration. Decommissioning procedures must include factory reset or physical destruction of storage, and revocation of any credentials the device held.

learning_objectives: Analyze the Purdue Model and its application to ICS network segmentation
Identify vulnerabilities in industrial protocols including Modbus, DNP3, and OPC UA
Design defense-in-depth architectures for operational technology environments
Evaluate the security implications of IT/OT convergence
readings: NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3 - Guide to OT Security
ICS-CERT advisories and case studies (Colonial Pipeline, Stuxnet, TRITON)
ISA/IEC 62443 Industrial Automation and Control Systems Security Standards

lecture_notes.introduction: Industrial control systems and SCADA networks manage the physical infrastructure that society depends on: power grids, water treatment plants, manufacturing facilities, oil and gas pipelines, and transportation systems. The security of these systems has implications far beyond data loss. A successful attack on an ICS can cause physical damage, environmental contamination, or loss of human life. Historically, ICS networks were air-gapped from corporate IT networks and the internet, providing security through isolation. That isolation has eroded as organizations connect OT networks to IT systems for monitoring, data analytics, and remote management. This IT/OT convergence brings the full spectrum of cyber threats to environments that were never designed to defend against them. ICS protocols like Modbus, developed in 1979, have no authentication or encryption because they were designed for closed, trusted networks. Programmable logic controllers (PLCs) and remote terminal units (RTUs) often run proprietary real-time operating systems that cannot be patched without vendor involvement and scheduled downtime. The consequences of patching failures in ICS are also different: a patch that causes a reboot in an IT server is an inconvenience, but a patch that causes a reboot in a PLC controlling a chemical reactor could trigger a safety incident. The Purdue Model provides a hierarchical framework for segmenting ICS networks into zones with controlled data flows between layers, and ISA/IEC 62443 defines security requirements for industrial automation. This session examines how to secure these critical systems given their unique constraints.
key_concepts: Purdue Model levels: Level 0 (physical process), Level 1 (basic control - PLCs/RTUs), Level 2 (area supervisory - HMIs/SCADA), Level 3 (site operations - historians/MES), Level 3.5 (DMZ), Levels 4-5 (enterprise IT)

ICS protocol vulnerabilities: Modbus TCP has no authentication or encryption; DNP3 Secure Authentication adds HMAC but is not widely deployed; OPC UA provides modern security but legacy devices cannot upgrade
IT/OT convergence risks: connecting OT networks to IT for remote monitoring exposes ICS to ransomware, credential theft, and lateral movement from compromised IT systems

Defense-in-depth for ICS: unidirectional security gateways (data diodes) allowing data out of OT but preventing any traffic into OT, ICS-specific firewalls with protocol-aware inspection, and network monitoring tuned for ICS protocols

ICS-specific threats: TRITON/TRISIS targeted safety instrumented systems (SIS) to disable safety shutdowns; Stuxnet destroyed centrifuges by altering PLC programming; Colonial Pipeline ransomware shut down fuel distribution

Patch management in OT: patches require vendor validation, scheduled downtime windows (often quarterly or annual), and rollback plans; virtual patching through ICS-aware IPS rules provides interim protection
ISA/IEC 62443 zones and conduits: defining security zones with common security requirements and conduits (controlled communication paths) between zones with explicit access policies

Monitoring OT networks: passive network monitoring that does not inject traffic (critical in environments where unexpected packets can disrupt real-time control), asset discovery, and protocol-aware anomaly detection
Safety system independence: safety instrumented systems (SIS) must be isolated from control networks to prevent an attacker who compromises the control system from disabling safety protections
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a simulated ICS environment using GRFICSv2 (a virtual ICS training platform), demonstrating a Modbus command injection attack that alters PLC values, then implementing network monitoring with Zeek to detect the anomalous Modbus commands and configuring firewall rules based on the Purdue Model to prevent the attack
setup_requirements: GRFICSv2 virtual ICS environment
Modbus TCP client tools for command injection simulation
Zeek network monitor with ICS protocol parsers
Network diagram template for Purdue Model design

review.value: In IT, confidentiality is often the top priority. In OT, availability and safety come first. A water treatment plant must continue operating; the risk of shutting it down for patching may exceed the risk of the vulnerability. Security controls must not compromise safety or uptime.

The Level 3.5 DMZ is the critical boundary between IT and OT. All data exchange between enterprise IT and industrial control systems must flow through the DMZ. Direct connections from IT to OT bypass all protection and create a direct attack path to physical processes.

Standard IT IDS rules do not understand Modbus or DNP3. ICS-aware monitoring tools parse industrial protocols to detect abnormal commands (writing unexpected values to PLC registers, issuing emergency shutdown commands) that would appear as normal TCP traffic to IT security tools.

Unidirectional security gateways physically enforce one-way data flow: OT data can be sent to IT for analysis, but no data can flow back. This provides the strongest possible protection against remote attacks on ICS while preserving monitoring capability.

TRITON demonstrated that if safety systems are reachable from the control network, an attacker can disable them before triggering a dangerous condition. Safety instrumented systems must have independent network connectivity that cannot be reached from the process control network.

class_schedule.topic: Encrypted Traffic Analysis and Threat Detection
learning_objectives: Apply metadata analysis techniques to detect threats in encrypted traffic without decryption
Implement JA3/JA3S TLS fingerprinting for malware identification
Design encrypted traffic analytics (ETA) systems using flow-level features
Evaluate the tradeoffs between TLS inspection and metadata-based analysis
readings: Machine Learning for Encrypted Traffic Analysis by Anderson and McGrew (Cisco research papers)
JA3 and JA3S TLS fingerprinting documentation
Cisco Encrypted Traffic Analytics whitepaper

lecture_notes.introduction: The widespread adoption of TLS encryption presents a fundamental challenge for network security: the majority of network traffic is now opaque to traditional inspection tools. Over 95% of web traffic is encrypted with HTTPS, and malware authors have adopted TLS to blend their command-and-control traffic with legitimate encrypted communications. The traditional solution, TLS interception via man-in-the-middle proxies, breaks end-to-end encryption, introduces performance overhead, creates privacy concerns, and does not work for certificate-pinned applications or protocols like QUIC. Encrypted traffic analysis (ETA) represents an alternative approach that extracts security-relevant insights from encrypted traffic without decryption. Even when the payload is encrypted, substantial metadata remains visible: TLS handshake parameters, certificate information, DNS queries, connection timing, data volume patterns, and flow sequences. Research has demonstrated that these metadata features are sufficient to identify the application generating the traffic, detect known malware families, and identify anomalous behavior patterns consistent with compromise. JA3 fingerprinting, which hashes the TLS ClientHello parameters into a compact fingerprint, can identify specific client implementations (and by extension, specific malware families) based on their unique TLS configuration. Flow-level analytics examine patterns across multiple connections: beacon-like regularity in connection timing, small consistent payload sizes suggesting C2 heartbeats, and data volume asymmetries suggesting exfiltration. This session covers the practical implementation of encrypted traffic analysis, demonstrating that meaningful security monitoring is possible even when payload inspection is not.
key_concepts: TLS handshake metadata: the ClientHello and ServerHello messages are sent in plaintext before encryption begins, revealing cipher suites, extensions, supported versions, and server certificate information

JA3 fingerprinting: hashing the TLS ClientHello fields (SSL version, cipher suites, extensions, elliptic curves, EC point formats) into an MD5 hash that uniquely identifies the TLS client implementation
JA3S fingerprinting: the server-side complement to JA3, hashing the ServerHello response; the JA3+JA3S pair identifies both ends of the TLS negotiation for more precise identification

Certificate analysis: examining certificate issuer, subject, validity period, SAN entries, and certificate chain for indicators of malicious infrastructure (self-signed, recently issued, free CA, short validity)

Flow-based behavioral analysis: examining connection timing patterns (beaconing regularity), data volume patterns (exfiltration vs heartbeat), session duration distributions, and connection concurrency
DNS correlation: pairing DNS queries with subsequent TLS connections to identify domain-fronting, DNS-over-HTTPS tunneling, and connections to dynamically generated domains (DGA detection)
HASSH fingerprinting: applying the JA3 concept to SSH handshakes, fingerprinting SSH client and server implementations to detect unauthorized SSH tools or anomalous client behavior

Machine learning for ETA: training classifiers on labeled encrypted traffic to identify application types, malware families, and anomalous patterns using features extracted from flow metadata without payload inspection
Limitations of ETA: encrypted traffic analysis cannot replace content inspection for all use cases; it cannot detect threats embedded in encrypted payloads that do not exhibit behavioral anomalies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an encrypted traffic analysis pipeline using Zeek to extract TLS metadata and flow features, computing JA3 fingerprints, matching them against a database of known malware fingerprints, and applying a trained random forest classifier to identify suspicious encrypted flows in a mixed traffic PCAP
setup_requirements: Zeek network monitor with JA3 plugin installed
JA3 fingerprint database (ja3er.com dataset)
Python with scikit-learn for flow classification
PCAP files containing labeled malicious and benign encrypted traffic

review.value: Research shows that TLS metadata alone can identify malware with over 99% accuracy for known families. The key features are JA3 fingerprint, certificate metadata, DNS query patterns, and connection behavior. You do not need to decrypt traffic to detect most threats.

C2 malware communicates at regular intervals (beaconing). Even with encrypted traffic and randomized payloads, the timing regularity is detectable. Calculate inter-connection intervals and flag connections with low timing variance, especially to uncommon destinations.

Attackers route C2 traffic through legitimate CDN domains so the TLS SNI and DNS query show a trusted domain while the HTTP Host header (encrypted) routes to the attacker. DNS-to-connection correlation and JA3 analysis can help detect this technique.

ETA and TLS inspection are not mutually exclusive. Use TLS inspection for traffic where content analysis is required (DLP, regulatory compliance) and ETA for traffic where decryption is impractical or inappropriate (certificate-pinned applications, personal devices, high-volume traffic).

Sophisticated attackers randomize JA3 fingerprints, use legitimate cloud services for C2, and add jitter to beaconing intervals. Detection must evolve beyond single indicators to multi-feature behavioral models that are harder to evade.

learning_objectives: Develop hypothesis-driven threat hunting methodologies based on MITRE ATT&CK
Use network telemetry sources including NetFlow, DNS logs, and proxy logs for proactive hunting
Build threat hunting queries and analytics for detecting living-off-the-land techniques
Establish a repeatable threat hunting program with metrics and feedback loops
readings: The Threat Hunting Playbook by Sqrrl (now Amazon)
MITRE ATT&CK techniques for network-based detection
SANS Threat Hunting Survey annual report

lecture_notes.introduction: Threat hunting is the proactive search for adversaries who have bypassed automated detection systems and are operating undetected within an environment. Unlike incident response, which is reactive and triggered by alerts, threat hunting starts with a hypothesis about adversary behavior and actively searches for evidence of that behavior in security telemetry. The fundamental assumption of threat hunting is that automated detection will miss some attacks, especially sophisticated adversaries using living-off-the-land techniques, stolen credentials, and encrypted channels. A 2024 study found that the median dwell time for undetected intrusions was 10 days in organizations with active threat hunting programs versus 204 days in organizations without. The threat hunting process follows a cycle: generate a hypothesis based on threat intelligence (for example, APT29 has been targeting our industry sector using compromised OAuth tokens), identify the data sources where evidence of this technique would appear (Azure AD sign-in logs, OAuth token usage patterns), develop queries or analytics to search for the hypothesized behavior, investigate any matches, and feed findings back into automated detection. Network telemetry is a rich source for threat hunting because it provides visibility into communication patterns that endpoint detection may miss: beaconing to C2 infrastructure, data staging and exfiltration, lateral movement between systems, and DNS tunneling for covert channels. This session covers the methodology, tools, and practical techniques for building an effective network threat hunting capability.
key_concepts: Hypothesis-driven hunting: starting with a specific, testable hypothesis about adversary behavior rather than generic searches; hypotheses come from threat intelligence, incident reports, and MITRE ATT&CK techniques

Hunting data sources: NetFlow/IPFIX for connection metadata, DNS logs for domain resolution patterns, proxy logs for web activity, firewall logs for blocked connections, and full packet capture for deep investigation
MITRE ATT&CK for hunters: using the framework to systematically generate hypotheses for each tactic, identifying techniques relevant to your environment and developing detection queries for each

Living-off-the-land network indicators: PowerShell remoting (WinRM on port 5985/5986), WMI over DCOM, PsExec SMB traffic, and RDP connections between non-standard hosts that indicate legitimate tools used for malicious purposes

Beacon detection analytics: statistical analysis of connection intervals to identify regular callback patterns, calculating jitter ratios, and applying frequency analysis to time-series connection data

DNS hunting: identifying DGA domains through entropy analysis, detecting DNS tunneling through unusually long subdomain labels or high query volumes to single domains, and correlating DNS queries with threat intelligence

Data staging and exfiltration indicators: identifying unusual outbound data volumes from servers that normally only serve requests, connections to cloud storage services from non-standard hosts, and compressed archive creation followed by outbound transfers

Hunt documentation and feedback: every hunt should produce a report documenting the hypothesis, methodology, findings, and recommended detection improvements; successful hunts become automated detection rules

Threat hunting maturity model: from ad-hoc hunting based on articles to structured programs with dedicated hunters, integrated threat intelligence, and automated analytics that continuously scan for known hunt patterns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a structured threat hunt for DNS tunneling using Zeek DNS logs: calculating subdomain entropy for all DNS queries over 24 hours, identifying domains with anomalously high entropy or query frequency, investigating the top candidates, and creating a Suricata rule to automatically detect the pattern going forward
setup_requirements: Zeek DNS logs from a simulated enterprise network
Python scripts for entropy calculation and statistical analysis
DNS tunneling tool (iodine or dnscat2) for generating test traffic
Suricata for creating automated detection rules

review.value: Threat hunting starts where automated detection ends. Hunters look for adversary behaviors that did not trigger alerts: subtle patterns, low-and-slow operations, and techniques that blend with legitimate traffic. If your hunt only finds things that already generated alerts, you are not hunting.

A vague hypothesis like 'look for bad stuff in DNS logs' produces noise. A specific hypothesis like 'APT41 uses DNS TXT queries to base64-encoded subdomains exceeding 50 characters for data exfiltration' produces focused, testable analysis.

Every successful hunt should result in a new automated detection rule. The hunt proved the adversary behavior is detectable in your telemetry. Convert the hunting query into a SIEM rule, IDS signature, or SOAR playbook so you do not need to manually hunt for it again.

Endpoint detection can be evaded by sophisticated attackers who disable or blind EDR agents. Network telemetry is harder to tamper with because the adversary does not control the network infrastructure. DNS queries, connection metadata, and traffic patterns persist even when endpoint visibility is compromised.

You cannot find anomalies without knowing what is normal. Hunters need deep familiarity with the environment: what DNS servers are used, what IP ranges are internal, what outbound traffic patterns are normal, and what administrative tools are legitimately deployed.

learning_objectives: Plan and execute red team network operations including initial access, lateral movement, and exfiltration
Use network-focused attack tools including Responder, CrackMapExec, and Impacket
Develop covert command-and-control channels that evade network detection
Document red team findings in actionable reports that drive defensive improvements
readings: Red Team Development and Operations by Joe Vest and James Tubberville
Impacket library documentation and attack examples
MITRE ATT&CK network-based techniques for red teams

lecture_notes.introduction: Red team operations simulate real-world adversary behavior to test an organization's detection and response capabilities. Unlike penetration testing, which focuses on finding vulnerabilities, red teaming focuses on achieving objectives while evading detection, testing the full security stack from prevention controls through detection systems to incident response processes. Network operations are central to red team engagements because nearly every attack involves network communication: initial access through phishing or external exploitation, establishing command-and-control channels, moving laterally between systems, and exfiltrating data. A skilled red team operator must understand network security from the attacker's perspective: which protocols are monitored, what detection rules exist, how to blend malicious traffic with legitimate network activity, and how to maintain persistent access without triggering alerts. Modern red team network operations have evolved significantly beyond basic port scanning and exploitation. Teams now use domain fronting through CDN providers, DNS-over-HTTPS for covert channels, legitimate cloud services for C2 relay, and protocol-specific evasion techniques that exploit gaps in network monitoring. The defenders' perspective is equally important: understanding red team techniques enables blue teams to build detection strategies that cover the actual attack surface rather than theoretical threats. This session covers the offensive network techniques that red teams employ and the detection opportunities they create.
key_concepts: Red team engagement scoping: defining objectives (access to specific data, systems, or network zones), rules of engagement (authorized networks, time windows, prohibited actions), and success criteria that align with organizational risk concerns
Initial access techniques: spear-phishing with macro-enabled documents, external service exploitation, supply chain compromise, and credential stuffing against exposed authentication portals
Command-and-control architectures: malleable C2 profiles (Cobalt Strike), DNS-based C2 (dnscat2), HTTPS-based C2 with domain fronting, and serverless C2 using cloud functions as redirectors
Lateral movement via network protocols: NTLM relay attacks using Responder, Pass-the-Hash/Pass-the-Ticket with Impacket, WMI and PowerShell remoting, and PsExec-style SMB-based execution
Network credential harvesting: LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning with Responder to capture NTLMv2 hashes, ARP spoofing for traffic interception, and Kerberoasting for service account password cracking

Data exfiltration techniques: chunked exfiltration over DNS, steganography in image uploads to legitimate services, encrypted archive upload to cloud storage, and slow-rate exfiltration that stays below volume-based detection thresholds

Covert channel design: using allowed protocols (HTTPS, DNS) as transport for C2 traffic, timing-based channels that encode data in connection intervals, and protocol-compliant traffic that passes deep packet inspection

Evasion of network detection: traffic shaping to avoid beacon detection (jitter, randomized intervals), rotating C2 domains and IP addresses, using legitimate cloud infrastructure as relay points, and mimicking normal user traffic patterns
Red team reporting: documenting the attack path with timestamps, detection gaps identified, specific recommendations for each gap, and risk ratings based on the impact of each finding
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a controlled red team exercise in the lab: using Responder to capture NTLMv2 hashes via LLMNR poisoning, cracking captured hashes with Hashcat, using the credentials with CrackMapExec for lateral movement via SMB, and establishing a covert DNS C2 channel with dnscat2, while simultaneously monitoring all activity from the defender's perspective using Zeek and Suricata
setup_requirements: Isolated lab network with Active Directory domain
Responder for LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning
CrackMapExec and Impacket for lateral movement
dnscat2 for DNS-based C2 demonstration

review.value: Penetration testing asks: can we find vulnerabilities? Red teaming asks: can we achieve this objective without being detected? The red team tests detection and response capabilities, not just preventive controls. The value is in identifying what the blue team misses.

When a DNS lookup fails, Windows falls back to LLMNR and NBT-NS multicast protocols. Responder answers these broadcasts, directing the victim to the attacker, who captures NTLMv2 authentication hashes. Defense: disable LLMNR and NBT-NS via Group Policy.

Effective C2 channels use allowed protocols, mimic legitimate traffic patterns, rotate infrastructure, and include fallback communication methods. The most common evasion technique is using HTTPS to a domain hosted on a major CDN, making the traffic indistinguishable from normal web browsing.

The best red team operators understand blue team detection capabilities. Knowing what the SIEM rules look for, what traffic the IDS inspects, and what endpoints EDR monitors allows targeted evasion and identifies the most impactful detection gaps.

A red team report that says 'we got domain admin' without explaining how and what to fix is useless. Each finding must include: the technique used, the detection gap exploited, the specific detection improvement that would catch it, and the priority based on risk.

learning_objectives: Execute structured defense drills responding to simulated network attacks in real-time
Operate a security operations center with defined roles and communication procedures
Perform network forensic analysis during active incidents to identify scope and impact
Develop and execute containment strategies that minimize business disruption
readings: Blue Team Handbook: SOC, SIEM, and Threat Hunting Use by Don Murdoch
NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 - Computer Security Incident Handling Guide
Security Operations Center: Building, Operating, and Maintaining Your SOC by Muniz et al.

lecture_notes.introduction: Blue team defense drills translate theoretical knowledge into practical skill under pressure. Knowing how to analyze a PCAP in a classroom is fundamentally different from identifying and containing an active attack while alerts are firing, stakeholders are asking questions, and the attacker is still operating. Effective incident response requires not just technical skill but also communication, coordination, and decision-making under uncertainty. A security operations center operates as a team: the triage analyst performs initial alert investigation and escalation, the incident handler coordinates the response and communicates with management, the forensic analyst performs deep technical investigation, and the network analyst traces the attacker's path through network telemetry. Each role has defined responsibilities, and the team must communicate continuously to maintain shared situational awareness. Defense drills simulate realistic attack scenarios in a controlled environment where the blue team practices detection, investigation, containment, and eradication. Unlike tabletop exercises that discuss hypothetical scenarios, these drills involve actual attack traffic on real (lab) infrastructure, requiring the team to use production tools and techniques under time pressure. This session puts students in the SOC with defined roles, facing a multi-phase simulated attack that requires coordinated defense. The attack scenario includes initial compromise through a phishing payload, lateral movement using stolen credentials, privilege escalation to domain administrator, and data staging for exfiltration. The blue team must detect each phase, investigate scope, contain the threat, and preserve forensic evidence.
key_concepts: SOC role definitions: Tier 1 triage analyst (initial alert review, false positive filtering), Tier 2 incident handler (deep investigation, containment decisions), Tier 3 threat hunter/forensic analyst (root cause analysis, advanced investigation)
Incident command structure: incident commander (decision authority), communications officer (stakeholder updates), technical lead (directs investigation), scribe (documents actions and timeline)
Network forensic analysis during incidents: packet capture analysis, NetFlow timeline reconstruction, DNS query history, authentication log correlation, and lateral movement path mapping

Containment strategies: network isolation (VLAN change, firewall block, SDN quarantine), credential invalidation (password reset, token revocation), and endpoint isolation (EDR quarantine, network disconnection)

Evidence preservation: full packet capture of attacker traffic, system memory acquisition before remediation, log preservation with timestamps, and chain-of-custody documentation for potential legal proceedings

Communication during incidents: structured status updates at defined intervals, clear escalation criteria, management briefings with business impact focus, and external communication coordination for regulatory notification
Attacker dwell time indicators: identifying how long the adversary has been present by tracing the earliest evidence of compromise in historical logs, DNS queries, and authentication records

Eradication verification: confirming that all attacker footholds (persistence mechanisms, additional accounts, implants on other systems) have been identified and removed before declaring the incident resolved
Post-incident review: conducting a blameless retrospective focused on detection time, response actions, communication effectiveness, and specific improvements for each gap identified
lecture_notes.demonstration: Executing a live defense drill where the instructor operates as the red team executing a pre-scripted multi-phase attack while students operate the SOC in defined roles, using Zeek, Suricata, and Elastic SIEM to detect and respond to the attack in real-time, with debriefing after each phase to discuss detection opportunities and response decisions
setup_requirements: Lab environment with Active Directory, web servers, and database servers
Elastic SIEM with Zeek and Suricata log ingestion
Pre-scripted attack scenario with defined phases and timing
SOC role assignment cards with responsibilities and procedures

review.value: Drills that are too easy or too scripted build false confidence. Effective drills include realistic noise (false positives), time pressure, incomplete information, and attacker actions that adapt to the defenders' responses. Discomfort during drills means learning is happening.

Most drill failures are communication failures, not technical failures. The analyst who detected the threat did not tell the handler, the handler did not escalate to management, or containment was delayed because no one had the authority to act. Practice communication under pressure.

Fast containment (immediately disconnect the compromised host) stops the attacker but may lose volatile evidence. Thorough containment (capture memory, full packet capture, then isolate) preserves evidence but gives the attacker more time. The right balance depends on the severity and the organization's priorities.

Incident response often destroys forensic evidence: reimaging a compromised system eliminates malware artifacts, rotating credentials before capturing authentication logs loses the attacker's access timeline. Preserve evidence first, then remediate.

Post-incident reviews that blame individuals discourage reporting and transparency. Focus on system improvements: what detection was missing, what process was unclear, what tool was insufficient. Every incident is an opportunity to improve the security program.

learning_objectives: Design automated incident response playbooks for common alert types
Integrate security tools through APIs to build automated workflows
Implement human-in-the-loop decision points for high-impact automated actions
Measure SOAR effectiveness through metrics including mean time to respond and analyst productivity
readings: SOAR platform documentation (Shuffle SOAR or Tines)
Gartner Market Guide for SOAR Solutions
SANS automation and orchestration survey

lecture_notes.introduction: Security operations centers face an asymmetric challenge: alert volumes grow exponentially while analyst headcount grows linearly at best. A typical enterprise SOC processes 10,000 to 50,000 alerts per day, and analysts can manually investigate 20 to 40 alerts per shift. Without automation, the vast majority of alerts go uninvestigated, creating blind spots that adversaries exploit. Security orchestration, automation, and response platforms address this gap by automating the repetitive, well-defined steps of incident response while preserving human judgment for complex decisions. SOAR platforms operate on the concept of playbooks: predefined workflows that specify the sequence of automated actions to take when a specific alert type is triggered. A phishing alert playbook might automatically extract URLs and attachments from the reported email, check them against threat intelligence, search for other users who received the same email, quarantine the email from all mailboxes, and create a case with all collected evidence for an analyst to review. The automation handles in seconds what would take an analyst 20 to 30 minutes of manual work across multiple tools. The key design principle of effective SOAR implementation is knowing where to draw the line between automation and human judgment. Low-risk, high-confidence actions (enriching an IP address with threat intelligence, checking a file hash against VirusTotal) should be fully automated. High-impact actions (blocking a production IP address, disabling a user account, quarantining a server) should require human approval unless the confidence level is extremely high. This session covers the architecture, playbook design, tool integration, and operational metrics of SOAR platforms.
key_concepts: SOAR architecture: trigger engine (receives alerts from SIEM, email, tickets), orchestration engine (executes playbook steps in sequence or parallel), integration layer (APIs to security tools), case management (tracks investigations)

Playbook design patterns: linear (sequential steps), branching (decision trees based on enrichment results), parallel (multiple enrichments simultaneously), and looping (iterating over multiple indicators)
Tool integration via APIs: connecting SIEM, firewall, EDR, email gateway, threat intelligence, ticketing, and identity providers into automated workflows through REST APIs

Automated enrichment: IP/domain reputation lookup, file hash analysis, WHOIS information, geolocation, user account lookup, and asset criticality assessment as the foundation for automated decision-making
Human-in-the-loop design: automated actions up to the decision point, then presenting the analyst with enriched context and recommended action, requiring approval for high-impact responses
Common SOAR playbooks: phishing triage, malware alert response, brute force containment, compromised credential response, and vulnerability alert prioritization, each automating 80-90% of manual work
Mean Time to Respond (MTTR): the primary metric for SOAR effectiveness; compare MTTR before and after automation for each alert type to quantify the value of automation investment
Playbook testing and maintenance: playbooks are code and require testing, version control, and maintenance; API changes in integrated tools break playbooks; regression testing is essential
Alert fatigue reduction: SOAR reduces the number of alerts requiring human attention by auto-closing false positives and auto-resolving known-good patterns, focusing analyst time on genuine threats
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a SOAR playbook in Shuffle (open-source SOAR) that automatically responds to a phishing report: extracts email headers and body, detonates attachments in a sandbox (VirusTotal), checks URLs against threat intelligence, searches the email gateway for other recipients, and creates a case with all findings for analyst review, with automatic quarantine if the verdict is malicious
setup_requirements: Shuffle SOAR platform deployed on Docker
VirusTotal API key for file and URL analysis
Simulated email gateway API
Simulated SIEM for alert generation

review.key: Automate the Boring, Not the Important
review.value: Automate enrichment, context gathering, and evidence collection. Require human judgment for containment actions that could disrupt business operations. The analyst should spend time on decisions, not data collection.

A playbook that fails during a real incident is worse than no playbook because the team may not realize it failed and assume automated actions were taken. Test playbooks regularly with simulated alerts and verify that every step executes correctly.

The value of SOAR is directly proportional to the number and depth of tool integrations. Each additional integration (SIEM, firewall, EDR, email, identity) enables more comprehensive automated workflows. Budget time for building and maintaining integrations.

Track MTTR by alert type, false positive auto-closure rate, analyst time saved per playbook, and playbook failure rate. These metrics justify SOAR investment and identify which playbooks need improvement.

The threat landscape changes, tools update their APIs, and organizational processes evolve. Playbooks require regular review and updates. Assign playbook ownership to specific analysts responsible for maintaining and improving each playbook.

class_schedule.topic: Network Security Automation and Infrastructure as Code
learning_objectives: Implement network security policies as code using Terraform and Ansible
Design CI/CD pipelines for automated security policy deployment and testing
Build automated compliance checking for network security configurations
Develop self-healing security architectures that automatically remediate configuration drift
readings: Infrastructure as Code, 2nd Ed. by Kief Morris, Ch. 12-14
Terraform security provider documentation
Ansible network automation modules documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Manual network security configuration does not scale. An enterprise with hundreds of firewalls, thousands of security groups, and tens of thousands of access control entries cannot maintain consistent security posture through manual changes. Configuration drift, where deployed configurations gradually deviate from the intended state, is inevitable when humans manually manage security infrastructure. Infrastructure as code (IaC) applies software engineering practices to infrastructure management: security configurations are defined in version-controlled code, changes go through review processes, automated pipelines deploy configurations, and drift detection systems alert when reality diverges from the defined state. For network security, IaC means that firewall rules, security group policies, IDS configurations, and access control lists are all defined in declarative code rather than configured through web interfaces or CLI sessions. This approach provides several critical benefits: configuration changes are auditable through version control history, peer review of security changes catches errors before deployment, automated testing validates that changes do not break connectivity or create security gaps, and rollback to a known-good state is as simple as reverting a commit. The concept extends to self-healing architectures where automated systems continuously compare the deployed state against the declared state and automatically remediate any drift. If someone manually adds an insecure firewall rule, the automation detects the change and reverts it within minutes. This session covers the practical implementation of security automation, from defining security policies as code through building deployment pipelines to implementing continuous compliance monitoring.
key_concepts: Security policy as code: defining firewall rules, security groups, ACLs, and IDS configurations in declarative languages (Terraform HCL, Ansible YAML) that are version-controlled and peer-reviewed
Terraform for network security: managing cloud security groups, WAF rules, network ACLs, and firewall configurations as code with state management and plan-before-apply workflows
Ansible for security automation: using network modules to configure firewalls, switches, and IDS/IPS systems with idempotent playbooks that ensure desired state regardless of current state
