CI/CD for security policies: automated pipelines that lint security configurations, run compliance checks, test connectivity, deploy to staging, verify, and promote to production with approval gates
Automated compliance checking: tools that continuously compare deployed configurations against security baselines (CIS Benchmarks, organizational standards) and report or auto-remediate deviations
Drift detection and remediation: monitoring for unauthorized manual changes to security configurations and automatically reverting them to the declared state, with alerting for investigation
Policy testing: writing automated tests that verify security policies work as intended (blocked traffic is actually blocked, allowed traffic actually passes) before deploying to production
GitOps for network security: using Git as the single source of truth for all security configurations, with automated reconciliation ensuring the deployed state always matches the Git repository
Change management automation: automated change requests, approval workflows, deployment windows, and rollback procedures integrated into the deployment pipeline
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a Terraform configuration that defines AWS VPC security groups and network ACLs for a multi-tier application, creating a CI pipeline using GitHub Actions that runs security linting (tfsec), compliance checking (Checkov), and connectivity testing before deploying changes, then demonstrating how manually modifying a security group triggers drift detection and automated remediation
setup_requirements: Terraform with AWS provider configured
GitHub repository with Actions enabled
tfsec and Checkov security scanning tools
AWS free tier account with VPC and EC2 access

review.value: Every security configuration change must go through version control. This provides: an audit trail of who changed what and when, the ability to peer-review changes before deployment, easy rollback when changes cause problems, and a single source of truth for the intended security state.

Terraform's plan phase shows exactly what changes will be made before they are applied. Review the plan for unintended consequences: will this rule change block legitimate traffic? Will this security group modification expose a service? Never apply without reviewing the plan.

Automated tests should verify that firewall rules block what they claim to block. Deploy the policy to a test environment, run connectivity tests that verify blocked paths are actually blocked, and verify allowed paths still work. Policy errors in production cause outages or security gaps.

Manual changes made outside the IaC pipeline (emergency fixes, ad-hoc troubleshooting) create drift between the declared state and the actual state. Drift detection and automated remediation ensure that the security posture you defined is the security posture you have.

The ultimate goal is a security infrastructure that automatically corrects deviations. If an insecure configuration is manually applied, the automation detects it and reverts it within minutes. This makes security the default state, not a target that requires constant effort to maintain.

class_schedule.topic: Capstone: Integrated Network Security Architecture Lab
learning_objectives: Design and implement a comprehensive network security architecture integrating all course technologies
Defend against a multi-phase simulated attack using layered security controls
Demonstrate detection, containment, and remediation capabilities across the full attack lifecycle
Present and justify architectural decisions to a panel of evaluators
readings: Review all course materials and lab documentation
NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 for architecture alignment
SANS 20 Critical Security Controls implementation guide

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone lab is the culminating assessment for this course, requiring students to synthesize all technologies, techniques, and architectural principles covered during the semester into a cohesive, defense-in-depth network security architecture. Working in teams, students design, build, and defend a multi-zone enterprise network incorporating next-generation firewalls, IDS/IPS, SDN microsegmentation, zero trust access controls, encrypted traffic analysis, threat hunting capabilities, and automated response through SOAR playbooks. The network environment simulates a realistic enterprise with a DMZ hosting public-facing services, an internal corporate network with Active Directory, a production database tier, an OT/IoT segment, and cloud connectivity. Each team must justify their architectural decisions with reference to the threats they address and the detection capabilities they provide. The defensive architecture is then tested against a multi-phase red team scenario executed by the instructor, progressing through initial access, command and control establishment, credential theft, lateral movement, privilege escalation, data access, and attempted exfiltration. Teams are evaluated not only on preventing attack phases but on detection speed, response effectiveness, forensic evidence preservation, and the quality of their post-incident analysis. This capstone demonstrates mastery of the course material by requiring students to apply advanced network security concepts to a realistic, complex scenario under time pressure, exactly mirroring the challenges they will face as network security professionals. The lab spans two weeks, with this session covering architecture design review and the first phase of the red team scenario.
key_concepts: Defense-in-depth architecture: layering preventive controls (firewalls, microsegmentation), detective controls (IDS/IPS, ETA, threat hunting), and responsive controls (SOAR, automated containment) so that no single control failure leads to compromise
Architecture documentation: network diagrams, data flow matrices, security control inventory, detection coverage mapping to MITRE ATT&CK, and residual risk assessment for accepted limitations
Multi-zone design: implementing the Purdue Model concepts with IT zones (DMZ, corporate, management), OT zones (control, process), and cloud zones with explicit inter-zone policies

Detection coverage analysis: mapping deployed detection capabilities to MITRE ATT&CK techniques to identify and acknowledge coverage gaps, prioritizing based on threat intelligence for the organization's sector

Automated response integration: SOAR playbooks that trigger containment actions based on detection events, with graduated responses from alert enrichment through automated isolation to analyst escalation
Red team resilience: architecture designed to detect and contain attacks even when individual controls are bypassed, with multiple detection opportunities at each phase of the kill chain

Forensic readiness: log collection, retention policies, full packet capture capability, and evidence preservation procedures built into the architecture from the start rather than added after an incident
Post-incident analysis: structured root cause analysis, detection timeline reconstruction, response effectiveness assessment, and specific recommendations for architectural improvements
Presentation and defense: communicating technical architecture decisions to both technical and non-technical audiences, justifying resource allocation and explaining residual risk
lecture_notes.demonstration: Instructor-led review of example capstone architectures from previous semesters, discussing what worked well and what failed during red team testing, followed by team breakout sessions to finalize architecture designs before the live defense exercise
setup_requirements: Full lab environment with multi-zone network topology
All security tools from the semester deployed and configured
Red team attack scripts prepared by the instructor
Evaluation rubric distributed to all teams

review.value: Every architecture has gaps. The mark of a mature security professional is acknowledging those gaps, understanding why they exist (resource constraints, technology limitations, operational requirements), and having compensating controls or monitoring in place for known weaknesses.

A determined attacker will eventually bypass preventive controls. The architecture must be designed with the assumption that initial access will succeed, and the defensive value is in rapid detection and containment that limits the impact of the breach.

An architecture with 47 different security tools and hundreds of custom rules looks impressive on paper but is unmaintainable in practice. Choose tools that integrate well, cover multiple detection needs, and can be managed by the team that will operate them.

Trace the attack path from initial access to objective completion. At each step, ask: what control prevents this? What control detects this? How fast can we respond? The answer should not be 'nothing' at any step in the kill chain.

The capstone is a snapshot. Real security architectures evolve continuously based on new threats, new technologies, lessons from incidents, and changes to the business. Build the architecture with evolution in mind: modular components, standardized interfaces, and documented decision rationale.

quizzes.topic: Firewall Architectures, IDS/IPS, and SDN Security

questions.question: An enterprise deploys a next-generation firewall with TLS inspection enabled. During a security audit, they discover that 30% of their traffic bypasses TLS inspection because applications use certificate pinning. Explain what certificate pinning is, why it prevents TLS inspection, describe two alternative detection methods for the uninspected traffic, and recommend an architectural change that provides visibility without breaking certificate pinning.
questions.answer: Certificate pinning is a security mechanism where an application stores (pins) the expected server certificate or public key and rejects any other certificate, even if it is signed by a trusted CA. TLS inspection works by presenting a proxy-generated certificate signed by a corporate CA installed on client devices. Pinned applications reject this proxy certificate because it does not match the pinned certificate, causing connection failures, so these applications are exempted from TLS inspection. Alternative detection methods: (1) JA3/JA3S fingerprinting of the TLS handshake metadata to identify the client application and detect known malware fingerprints without decrypting content. (2) Flow-based behavioral analysis examining connection timing patterns, data volume, and destination reputation to identify suspicious communication patterns in the encrypted traffic. Architectural recommendation: deploy encrypted traffic analytics (ETA) that operates on flow metadata and TLS handshake parameters rather than decrypted content. This provides detection capability for certificate-pinned traffic without breaking the application's security model. Combine with DNS monitoring to correlate certificate-pinned connections with domain lookups for additional context.

rubric.key: Correctly explains certificate pinning and why it defeats TLS inspection
Describes two valid alternative detection methods with technical accuracy
Provides a practical architectural recommendation

questions.question: Compare and contrast deploying an IDS in TAP/SPAN mode versus deploying an IPS inline. For each mode, describe the failure behavior, the impact on network latency, and the detection capabilities. Then recommend which mode should be used at the network perimeter versus between internal security zones, with justification.
questions.answer: TAP/SPAN (IDS) mode: The sensor receives a copy of traffic without being in the data path. Failure behavior: if the sensor crashes, traffic flow is unaffected (fail-safe for availability). Latency impact: zero, because traffic does not pass through the sensor. Detection capabilities: full detection and alerting, but cannot block traffic; can only generate alerts for response by other systems. Inline (IPS) mode: The sensor sits in the data path and all traffic passes through it. Failure behavior: depends on configuration; fail-open passes all traffic unfiltered (security gap) or fail-closed blocks all traffic (availability outage). Latency impact: adds 0.1-2ms per packet depending on rule complexity and traffic volume. Detection capabilities: full detection plus active blocking, can drop malicious packets in real-time. Recommendation: Network perimeter should use inline IPS because this is where blocking known threats has the highest value and the latency is acceptable for a single choke point. Between internal security zones, use TAP/SPAN mode for detection because: (1) latency accumulates across multiple hops and can affect application performance, (2) false positives in inline mode between internal zones cause production outages, and (3) internal traffic should be monitored for anomalies rather than blocked by signature, since most internal traffic is legitimate.

rubric.key: Accurately describes failure behavior for both modes
Correctly compares latency and detection capabilities
Provides justified deployment recommendation for perimeter vs internal

questions.question: An organization is deploying SDN with microsegmentation. The security team discovers that their physical switches support only 4,000 flow table entries, but their microsegmentation policy requires 12,000 rules. Describe the technical cause of this limitation, propose three strategies to reduce the number of flow table entries while maintaining security, and explain the risk of each strategy.
questions.answer: Technical cause: Physical switches store flow rules in TCAM (Ternary Content-Addressable Memory), which is expensive and power-hungry. TCAM capacity is fixed by hardware and cannot be expanded. Each microsegmentation rule (source workload + destination workload + port + action) consumes one or more TCAM entries. Strategy 1: Hierarchical policy aggregation. Group workloads by function (all web servers share one policy) and apply rules at the group level rather than per-workload. Risk: reduces granularity; compromise of one web server gives access to all web servers in the group. Strategy 2: Proactive flow rule installation. Only install flow rules for active communications rather than all possible rules. The controller installs rules on-demand when traffic is first observed. Risk: first-packet latency increases as each new flow requires a controller round-trip; during a burst of new connections, the controller may become a bottleneck. Strategy 3: Split enforcement between network and host. Use SDN flow rules for inter-zone policies (coarse segmentation) and host-based firewalls (iptables) for intra-zone microsegmentation. Risk: host-based firewall can be disabled if the host is compromised; two enforcement systems must be kept in sync, increasing management complexity.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies TCAM as the limiting factor
Proposes three valid strategies for reducing flow table entries
Accurately identifies risks for each strategy

questions.question: Explain the key differences between a traditional VPN-based remote access architecture and a zero trust network access (ZTNA) architecture. For each, describe what happens after a user authenticates, what network resources they can reach, and how the architecture responds to a compromised user device.
questions.answer: VPN-based architecture: After authentication, the user's device is connected to the corporate network via an encrypted tunnel. The device receives a corporate IP address and can reach any resource on the network that is not blocked by internal firewalls. If the device is compromised, the attacker has network-level access to the entire corporate network, can scan for vulnerabilities, move laterally, and access any service reachable from the VPN subnet. Detection depends on the compromised device generating suspicious network traffic that triggers IDS alerts. ZTNA architecture: After authentication, the user is granted access to specific applications, not to the network. The user's device connects to an identity-aware proxy or cloud-based ZTNA broker that mediates access on a per-application basis. Each application access request is evaluated against the user's identity, device posture (patch level, endpoint protection status), and contextual factors (location, time, risk score). If the device is compromised, the attacker can only reach the specific applications the user was authorized for, not the broader network. If device posture changes (EDR detects malware), the ZTNA policy engine can immediately revoke access to all applications. The attacker cannot scan the network or discover other resources because they have no network-level connectivity. Key difference: VPN trusts the network location; ZTNA trusts the verified identity and device state. VPN grants broad access once; ZTNA continuously validates before each access decision.

rubric.key: Accurately describes post-authentication behavior for both architectures
Correctly explains the scope of accessible resources for each
Describes compromised device scenario accurately for both

quizzes.topic: Zero Trust, Microsegmentation, and Wireless Security

questions.question: A hospital network has three segments: medical devices (MRI machines, infusion pumps), clinical workstations, and electronic health records (EHR) servers. Design a microsegmentation policy for this environment. Specify what traffic should be allowed between each pair of segments and what should be blocked, considering both security requirements and clinical workflow needs. Explain how your design complies with HIPAA network segmentation requirements.
questions.answer: Medical Devices to EHR Servers: BLOCKED for direct access. Medical devices should send data through a clinical middleware gateway that translates device protocols (HL7, DICOM) and sanitizes data before writing to the EHR. Rationale: medical devices run legacy OS that cannot be patched and are a high compromise risk; direct database access would allow a compromised device to read or modify all patient records. Medical Devices to Clinical Workstations: BLOCKED for initiated connections from devices; ALLOW clinical workstations to connect to device management interfaces on specific ports only. Rationale: clinicians need to view device data, but devices should not initiate connections to workstations. Clinical Workstations to EHR Servers: ALLOW on specific application ports (HTTPS/443 for EHR web interface, HL7 FHIR API ports) with user authentication. BLOCK direct database ports (1433, 5432). Rationale: clinicians need EHR access through the application layer, not direct database access. HIPAA compliance: HIPAA Security Rule requires that ePHI access be limited to authorized users and systems. Microsegmentation enforces technical access controls that complement administrative controls (user authentication). Audit logs of all inter-segment traffic provide the required access monitoring. The middleware gateway serves as a security boundary that limits the blast radius if a medical device is compromised, protecting ePHI from unauthorized access.

rubric.key: Correct traffic policies for all three segment pairs
Clinical workflow preserved while maintaining security
HIPAA compliance explanation is accurate

questions.question: Your organization discovers a rogue wireless access point connected to the corporate network in a conference room. It is broadcasting the same SSID as the corporate WiFi with WPA2-PSK. Explain how this attack works, what data the attacker can capture, and describe the specific technical controls that would prevent this attack and detect it if prevention fails.
questions.answer: Attack mechanism: The rogue AP broadcasts the corporate SSID with a stronger signal than the legitimate AP in the conference room. Client devices automatically connect to the strongest signal for the known SSID. Since it uses WPA2-PSK with the corporate password (which the attacker obtained or guessed), the connection appears legitimate to the client. Data captured: The attacker controls the network path and can perform man-in-the-middle attacks. They can capture DNS queries, unencrypted traffic, and with SSL stripping or certificate manipulation, potentially intercept credentials for sites without HSTS. They can also redirect clients to phishing pages. Prevention controls: (1) Deploy 802.1X/EAP-TLS authentication instead of WPA2-PSK. With EAP-TLS, the client authenticates the server certificate before sending credentials. The rogue AP cannot present a valid server certificate signed by the corporate CA, so the client rejects the connection. (2) Implement 802.1X port authentication on all Ethernet ports so the rogue AP cannot connect to the corporate network even if physically plugged in. Detection controls: (1) Deploy WIDS sensors that continuously scan the RF spectrum for access points broadcasting the corporate SSID that are not in the authorized AP inventory. Alert on any unauthorized SSID match. (2) Monitor switch port MAC addresses and alert on new devices connected to ports in public areas.

rubric.key: Correctly explains the rogue AP attack mechanism
Accurately describes data capture capabilities
Provides specific prevention and detection controls

questions.question: A CISO asks you to implement zero trust for their organization of 5,000 employees. They want to start with one pilot use case. Recommend a specific first pilot application, explain why it is a good starting point, describe the minimum infrastructure components needed, and outline the success criteria for the pilot.
questions.answer: Recommended pilot: Internal HR/payroll application (e.g., Workday or internal HR portal). Why it is a good starting point: (1) It has a well-defined user population (HR team + all employees for self-service), making policy creation manageable. (2) It contains sensitive PII data, providing clear security justification for the investment. (3) It is a web application accessible via HTTPS, which is the simplest application type for ZTNA integration. (4) It is not mission-critical for real-time operations, so any access issues during migration are manageable. Minimum infrastructure: (1) Identity provider with MFA (Okta, Azure AD) configured for all HR application users. (2) ZTNA access proxy (Zscaler Private Access, Cloudflare Access, or open-source Pomerium) positioned between users and the HR application. (3) Device posture assessment agent that checks OS patch level and endpoint protection status. (4) Conditional access policies: require MFA, require managed device for PII access, allow read-only from personal devices. Success criteria: (1) 100% of HR application access goes through the ZTNA proxy with no direct network access path. (2) Access failures for unauthorized users or non-compliant devices are logged and occur within 1 second. (3) No increase in IT help desk tickets related to HR application access after 30-day stabilization. (4) Successful demonstration that a user on an unmanaged device is restricted to read-only self-service functions.

rubric.key: Reasonable pilot application choice with justified rationale
Correctly identifies minimum infrastructure components
Specific, measurable success criteria

quizzes.topic: IoT Security, SCADA/ICS, and Encrypted Traffic Analysis

questions.question: An MQTT broker serving an IoT deployment of 500 smart building sensors is discovered to be running without authentication or TLS encryption on the corporate network. Describe the specific attacks an adversary on the same network can execute, the potential impact on building operations, and provide a step-by-step remediation plan that can be implemented without replacing the IoT devices.
questions.answer: Attacks possible: (1) Subscribe to all topics (# wildcard) to read every sensor reading (temperature, occupancy, access control events), gaining surveillance capability over the building. (2) Publish false data to sensor topics, causing HVAC systems to respond to fake temperature readings, potentially making areas uninhabitable or wasting energy. (3) Publish commands to actuator topics to unlock doors, disable alarms, or manipulate building systems. (4) Denial of service by flooding the broker with messages, disrupting legitimate sensor communication. Impact: Physical safety risk if HVAC or fire systems are manipulated. Security risk if access control is compromised. Operational disruption if building management systems receive false data. Privacy risk if occupancy sensors reveal employee movement patterns. Remediation plan (without device replacement): Step 1: Immediately move the MQTT broker to an isolated IoT VLAN with firewall rules blocking all traffic except from known IoT device IP addresses on the MQTT port. This contains the exposure within hours. Step 2: Enable TLS on the broker using a server certificate. Most MQTT client libraries support TLS even on constrained devices. Step 3: Enable username/password authentication on the broker and configure unique credentials per device or device class. Step 4: Implement topic-level ACLs so each device can only publish to its own topics and subscribe only to its command topics. Step 5: Deploy an MQTT-aware monitoring tool that logs all connections and publishes, alerting on subscription to wildcard topics or publications from unauthorized clients.

rubric.key: Identifies specific attacks with technical accuracy
Explains potential impact on building operations
Provides actionable remediation steps that work without device replacement

questions.question: Explain the Purdue Model for ICS network segmentation. A water treatment facility wants to allow remote monitoring of their SCADA system from the corporate IT network. Describe the correct architecture for enabling this monitoring while maintaining OT security, and explain what would happen if they instead created a direct VPN from corporate IT to the SCADA network.
questions.answer: The Purdue Model defines hierarchical levels: Level 0 (physical process - pumps, valves), Level 1 (basic control - PLCs/RTUs), Level 2 (area supervisory - HMIs/SCADA servers), Level 3 (site operations - historians, engineering workstations), Level 3.5 (DMZ between IT and OT), Levels 4-5 (enterprise IT and internet). Correct architecture for remote monitoring: A data historian or replica database is placed in the Level 3.5 DMZ. The SCADA system at Level 2 pushes data to the historian in the DMZ through a unidirectional gateway (data diode) that physically prevents any traffic from flowing from IT toward OT. Corporate IT users at Level 4 connect to the historian in the DMZ to view monitoring dashboards. They never have any network path to the actual SCADA system. The DMZ historian provides read-only access to operational data without creating a bidirectional connection to the OT network. Direct VPN from IT to SCADA consequences: A direct VPN creates a bidirectional network path from the corporate IT network to the SCADA system. If any corporate IT system is compromised by ransomware, a phishing attack, or an internet-facing vulnerability, the attacker can traverse the VPN connection directly to the SCADA system controlling water treatment. This is exactly how the 2021 Oldsmar water treatment plant incident and similar attacks occur. Malware like TRITON specifically targets safety systems once it reaches the OT network. The entire purpose of the Purdue Model DMZ is to prevent this scenario.

rubric.key: Correctly describes Purdue Model levels
Designs correct DMZ-based monitoring architecture
Explains the danger of direct IT-to-OT connectivity

questions.question: Your network monitoring team detects an internal host making HTTPS connections to a cloud storage provider every 300 seconds, transferring exactly 4KB each time. The traffic passes all firewall rules (cloud storage is allowed) and the TLS certificate is valid. Using only encrypted traffic analysis techniques (no decryption), describe how you would determine whether this traffic is legitimate or malicious, and what specific metadata features you would analyze.
questions.answer: Analysis approach using encrypted traffic metadata: (1) JA3 fingerprint: Extract the JA3 hash of the TLS ClientHello. Compare against known browser and application JA3 fingerprints. If the JA3 does not match any known legitimate application on that host, it may be a custom tool or malware. (2) Connection timing analysis: Calculate the inter-connection interval variance. Legitimate user activity has high variance (random browsing patterns). A 300-second interval with low jitter (less than 5% variance) is characteristic of automated beaconing. Plot the connection timestamps and calculate the coefficient of variation. (3) Payload size consistency: Legitimate cloud storage uploads vary widely in size. A consistent 4KB payload suggests a heartbeat or check-in message, not user-driven data upload. Compare against the baseline upload size distribution for this cloud service. (4) DNS resolution pattern: Check if the host resolves the cloud storage domain through corporate DNS or uses DNS-over-HTTPS. Check if the domain was first seen recently or has been accessed by other hosts. (5) Certificate analysis: Even though the certificate is valid, check the specific certificate details: is it the certificate used by the cloud provider's main service or a custom domain with a Let's Encrypt certificate hosted on the cloud platform? (6) Source process correlation: If EDR is available, correlate the connection times with the process making the connections. A browser process is expected; an unsigned executable or PowerShell script making regular cloud storage connections is suspicious. Determination: The combination of regular timing (beaconing), consistent small payload (heartbeat), and origin from a non-standard process would indicate malicious C2 communication using a legitimate cloud service as a relay.

rubric.key: Identifies relevant metadata features (JA3, timing, payload size, DNS)
Applies statistical analysis concepts to the metadata
Reaches a reasoned conclusion about malicious vs legitimate

quizzes.topic: Threat Hunting, Red Team Operations, and Blue Team Defense

questions.question: Design a threat hunt hypothesis for detecting credential theft via LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning in an enterprise network. Specify the hypothesis, the data sources you would search, the specific queries or analytics you would apply, and how you would distinguish between legitimate LLMNR traffic and Responder-based poisoning.
questions.answer: Hypothesis: An attacker is using Responder to poison LLMNR/NBT-NS broadcasts on the corporate network, capturing NTLMv2 authentication hashes from Windows clients that fail DNS resolution. Data sources: (1) Network traffic captures or Zeek logs showing LLMNR (UDP/5355) and NBT-NS (UDP/137) traffic. (2) Windows Security Event logs (Event ID 4624, 4625) showing authentication attempts. (3) DHCP logs showing IP address assignments for correlation. Analytics: (1) Identify all hosts responding to LLMNR queries. In a normal environment, LLMNR responses should come from the queried hostname. If a single IP address responds to LLMNR queries for many different hostnames, it is likely running Responder. (2) Correlate LLMNR responses with DHCP assignments: does the responding host's hostname match the name being queried? If host 10.0.1.50 (hostname WORKSTATION-5) responds to an LLMNR query for FILESERVER-1, this is poisoning. (3) Check for NTLM authentication attempts to unusual hosts: if many users authenticate (Event 4624/4625) to a host that is not a legitimate server, it is likely capturing credentials. Distinguishing legitimate from malicious: Legitimate LLMNR: the response comes from the host whose name was queried, and it resolves to that host's actual IP. Poisoning: the response comes from a different host claiming to be the queried name. Key indicator: one host answering LLMNR queries for many different names it does not own.

rubric.key: Specific, testable hypothesis
Correct data sources identified
Effective distinction between legitimate and malicious LLMNR

questions.question: During a red team engagement, the red team established C2 using HTTPS connections to a domain hosted on a major CDN (domain fronting). The blue team's IDS, proxy logs, and firewall logs all show the traffic going to the CDN's IP addresses with valid certificates. Explain why traditional detection methods fail for domain fronting, and describe two advanced detection techniques that could identify this traffic.
questions.answer: Why traditional detection fails: Domain fronting exploits the difference between the TLS SNI (Server Name Indication) and the HTTP Host header. The TLS handshake and DNS resolution show a connection to a legitimate domain (e.g., images.cdn-provider.com). The firewall sees a connection to a CDN IP address on port 443 with a valid certificate. The proxy log shows an HTTPS connection to a high-reputation domain. All of these appear completely legitimate. However, inside the encrypted HTTPS payload, the HTTP Host header directs the CDN to route the request to the attacker's backend server. Since the real destination is hidden inside the encrypted HTTP headers, no traditional network inspection reveals the true target. Detection technique 1: TLS-to-HTTP header comparison. If the organization performs TLS inspection for some traffic, compare the SNI domain in the TLS ClientHello with the Host header in the decrypted HTTP request. A mismatch (SNI says images.cdn-provider.com but Host header says attacker-c2.cdn-provider.com) is a strong indicator of domain fronting. For uninspected traffic, this comparison is not possible, limiting this technique. Detection technique 2: Behavioral analysis of CDN traffic patterns. Legitimate CDN usage shows diverse request patterns: many different URL paths, varying response sizes, and irregular timing. Domain fronting C2 exhibits regular beacon timing, consistent small request/response sizes, and repeated access to the same URL path. Statistical analysis of CDN connection patterns (timing regularity, payload size distribution, URL path entropy) can identify anomalous CDN traffic consistent with C2 communication even without decryption.

rubric.key: Correctly explains why domain fronting evades traditional detection
First detection technique is technically valid
Second detection technique is technically valid

questions.question: You are the incident commander during a blue team defense drill. Your Tier 1 analyst reports that Suricata generated an alert for lateral movement via PsExec from a workstation (10.0.1.100) to a domain controller (10.0.1.10). Describe your immediate response steps in order, what information you need before making containment decisions, and the specific containment actions you would authorize.
questions.answer: Immediate response steps in order: Step 1 (0-2 minutes): Verify the alert is not a false positive. Ask the Tier 1 analyst to confirm: Is PsExec a tool used by IT admins in this environment? Is 10.0.1.100 an IT admin workstation? Is this activity occurring during business hours? Check if there is a change management ticket for domain controller maintenance. Step 2 (2-5 minutes): If PsExec to a domain controller is unexpected, escalate immediately to Tier 2. This is a critical severity event because domain controller access means potential domain compromise. Step 3 (5-10 minutes): Gather context before containment. Information needed: (a) What user account executed PsExec? Is it a service account, admin account, or regular user? (b) What process on 10.0.1.100 launched PsExec? (c) Has 10.0.1.100 shown any other suspicious activity (alerts, unusual DNS, beaconing)? (d) Has the domain controller shown any changes (new accounts created, group membership changes, scheduled tasks)? Step 4: Containment decisions. If confirmed unauthorized: (a) Immediately disable the user account used for PsExec access (prevents further authenticated actions). (b) Isolate workstation 10.0.1.100 using EDR quarantine (maintains forensic state while preventing further lateral movement). (c) Do NOT immediately isolate the domain controller. Instead, capture its memory and monitor it closely. Isolating a DC causes widespread authentication failures. (d) Initiate a krbtgt password reset if there is evidence of Kerberos ticket theft. (e) Begin checking all other systems for similar PsExec activity from the compromised workstation to determine full scope.

rubric.key: Logical ordering of response steps
Identifies correct information needed before containment
Appropriate containment actions with DC-specific considerations

quizzes.topic: SOAR, Security Automation, and Integrated Defense

questions.question: Design a SOAR playbook for responding to a detection of DNS tunneling from an internal host. Include the trigger condition, at least five automated enrichment and response steps, two decision points requiring human judgment, and explain what artifacts the playbook should preserve for forensic analysis.
questions.answer: Trigger: SIEM alert when DNS analytics detect a host making more than 100 DNS queries per minute to a single domain with subdomain labels exceeding 30 characters (high entropy) for more than 5 minutes, indicating DNS tunneling for data exfiltration or C2. Automated steps: (1) Query DNS logs for the full history of the suspicious domain, collecting all subdomains queried and response records. (2) Look up the domain registration date, registrar, and hosting infrastructure. Recently registered domains on bulletproof hosting are higher risk. (3) Check the domain against threat intelligence feeds (VirusTotal, OTX, MISP). (4) Query the SIEM for all other internal hosts that have queried this domain to identify the full scope of potential compromise. (5) Capture the current DNS cache of the affected host and the DNS server query logs for evidence preservation. Decision point 1 (human judgment): If the domain is associated with a known legitimate service (e.g., a CDN or SaaS provider that uses long subdomain labels), an analyst must determine whether the traffic is a false positive or a legitimate service being abused as a tunneling channel. Decision point 2 (human judgment): Before blocking the domain at the DNS resolver, an analyst must verify that no legitimate business applications depend on this domain. Blocking a domain used by a critical application causes an outage. If both decisions confirm malicious activity: (6) Block the domain at the DNS resolver and firewall. (7) Isolate the source host via EDR quarantine. (8) Create an incident ticket with all enrichment data. Forensic artifacts to preserve: Complete DNS query log for the domain from the DNS server, full packet capture of DNS traffic from the affected host, memory dump of the affected host (the tunneling tool is likely running in memory), and the DNS resolver cache showing the resolved addresses.

rubric.key: Specific trigger condition with measurable thresholds
Five logical automated steps
Two appropriate human decision points
Correct forensic artifacts identified

questions.question: An organization manages 200 AWS security groups across 15 accounts using Terraform. A developer manually added a rule allowing SSH (port 22) from 0.0.0.0/0 to a production security group to debug an issue. Explain how infrastructure-as-code drift detection would identify this change, describe the automated remediation workflow, and discuss whether the remediation should be automatic or require approval.
questions.answer: Drift detection process: Terraform stores the desired state of all security groups in its state file, which reflects the last applied configuration from code. A scheduled pipeline (running every 15-30 minutes) executes 'terraform plan' against each account. The plan compares the desired state (code) with the actual state (AWS API). The plan output will show: 'security group sg-abc123 has an unexpected ingress rule: SSH from 0.0.0.0/0.' This drift is flagged as a security violation because the rule was not in the Terraform code. Automated remediation workflow: (1) The drift detection pipeline identifies the unauthorized rule. (2) An automated compliance check (Checkov or custom policy) classifies the drift as a critical security violation because it exposes SSH to the internet. (3) The pipeline triggers an alert to the security team with details: who owns the security group, what account, what VPC, and the specific rule added. (4) For critical violations (internet-exposed ports), the pipeline automatically applies 'terraform apply' to revert the security group to its declared state, removing the unauthorized SSH rule. (5) A notification is sent to both the security team and the developer who made the change, explaining that the manual change was reverted and providing instructions for requesting the change through the proper IaC process. Automatic vs approval: For critical security violations (0.0.0.0/0 on SSH, RDP, or database ports), remediation should be automatic with notification. The risk of an internet-exposed management port is immediate and severe (exploitation within minutes of exposure). For lower-severity drift (internal rule changes between private subnets), requiring approval is appropriate because the risk is lower and the change may be addressing a legitimate operational need that the IaC code needs to be updated to reflect.

rubric.key: Correctly explains Terraform drift detection mechanism
Describes a complete remediation workflow
Reasoned argument for when to auto-remediate vs require approval

questions.question: Explain how a defense-in-depth architecture provides resilience against a sophisticated attacker who bypasses the perimeter firewall. Using a scenario where the attacker gains initial access via a phishing email, describe the specific control that should detect or block the attack at each subsequent phase: C2 establishment, credential theft, lateral movement, privilege escalation, and data exfiltration.
questions.answer: Phishing delivers a macro-enabled document. Perimeter firewall is bypassed because the email arrives from a legitimate email service over HTTPS. C2 establishment: The malware connects to a C2 server. Detection: Encrypted traffic analytics identifies the new outbound connection with JA3 fingerprint matching known malware, or behavioral analysis detects regular beacon timing to an uncommon destination. IDS rules for known C2 infrastructure domains. Network monitoring detects DNS queries to newly registered domains. Credential theft: The attacker dumps credentials from memory using Mimikatz. Detection: EDR detects LSASS memory access by a non-system process. If EDR is evaded, the network control is monitoring for Kerberoasting traffic (TGS requests for service accounts with RC4 encryption) via IDS rules. Lateral movement: The attacker uses stolen credentials to move to another system via SMB or WinRM. Detection: Microsegmentation policies block the workstation from connecting to servers it does not normally access. SIEM correlation detects the stolen account authenticating from an unusual source host. Network IDS alerts on PsExec or WMI traffic between workstations. Privilege escalation: The attacker escalates to domain administrator. Detection: SIEM alerts on sensitive group membership changes (Domain Admins). Honey tokens (fake high-privilege accounts) trigger alerts when used. Network monitoring detects DCSync replication traffic from a non-domain-controller source. Data exfiltration: The attacker stages and exfiltrates sensitive data. Detection: DLP monitors detect sensitive data patterns in outbound traffic. Network monitoring detects unusual outbound data volumes from servers. DNS monitoring detects high-volume or high-entropy DNS queries indicating DNS tunneling exfiltration. Cloud access security broker (CASB) detects unusual uploads to cloud storage services.

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and defense drills. More than 2 unexcused absences results in a 5% grade reduction per additional absence. Lab-based sessions cannot be made up without instructor approval.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late up to 5 days. After 5 days, submissions are not accepted without documented extenuating circumstances approved by the instructor before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in assigned pairs. All written reports, quiz responses, and individual components must be your own work. Team assignments require individual contribution documentation.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All network security tools, scripts, and configurations must be your own work or properly attributed. Using pre-built attack scripts without understanding and documenting their operation is not acceptable. Unauthorized access to systems outside the lab environment will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a laptop capable of running VMware Workstation or VirtualBox with at least 16GB RAM and 100GB free disk space. Lab VMs will be provided. All testing must be conducted within the isolated lab environment.
